links.text: The Monument to the Great Fire of London — Wikipedia

summary: Sir Christopher Wren was an English architect of the seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries, best known for rebuilding 52 London churches after the Great Fire of 1666, most notably St. Paul's Cathedral on Ludgate Hill. He also designed the Monument to the Great Fire, the Royal Observatory at Greenwich, portions of Hampton Court Palace, and the library of Trinity College Cambridge. He was also a polymath who contributed illustrations to a seminal anatomy text.
comprehensive_summary:
Sir Christopher Wren was an English Baroque architect who became the preeminent builder of his era following the Great Fire of London in 1666. He was commissioned to redesign 52 London churches, with St. Paul's Cathedral — his masterpiece on Ludgate Hill — standing as his greatest achievement. Nikolaus Pevsner dubbed its dome 'the most perfect in the world.' Wren's tomb is contained within St. Paul's, and its Latin inscription reads: 'Reader, if you seek his monument, look around you.' The cathedral is an English Baroque edifice featuring woodwork by Grinling Gibbons, domes inspired by Mansart's Val-de-Grace, a facade with paired Corinthian columns, and a whispering gallery below its three-shelled dome. Herbert Mason's famous 1940 photograph of St. Paul's surrounded by Blitz smoke became an iconic image of resilience. Bill Viola's Martyrs and William Holman Hunt's The Light of the World are among the artworks housed inside.

Wren also designed the Monument to the Great Fire — a fluted Doric column 202 feet tall with an interior staircase allowing visitors to reach a golden urn of fire at the top. This monument was later bombed by Martial Bourdin in what has been described as the first international terrorist attack. Wren served as MP for Weymouth and used his access to Portland stone from the local quarries for St. Paul's.

The Royal Observatory at Greenwich was another Wren commission, built to house John Flamsteed's astronomical work; Flamsteed became its first resident. Tall windows were installed in the Octagon Room for the work. Greenwich also marks the prime meridian. Wren worked with Robert Hooke to reconstruct London after the Great Fire, and he also took on Nicholas Hawksmoor as an apprentice. He contributed illustrations to Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome, the book that introduced the word 'neurology.' Wren is sometimes credited with being a model for the Provincetown Heritage Museum, which appeared in Charles Demuth's watercolor After Sir Christopher Wren. He also designed the library at Trinity College Cambridge, portions of Hampton Court Palace (where William and Mary hoped to create an English rival of Versailles), the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich, and the Wren Building — the oldest collegiate building in the US — at the College of William and Mary. A false ceiling in Salisbury Cathedral conceals beams installed by Wren to help support the weight of its tower. John Dryden's poem Annus Mirabilis commemorates Wren's works.

works.name: St. Paul's Cathedral
works.indicator: Rebuilt after the Great Fire of London; whispering gallery; dome on Ludgate Hill
works.description: St. Paul's Cathedral on Ludgate Hill is Wren's most celebrated work, rebuilt after the Great Fire of 1666. Its dome — considered 'the most perfect in the world' by Nikolaus Pevsner — rises above a whispering gallery and features woodwork by Grinling Gibbons and a facade with paired Corinthian columns. Wren's tomb is inside, with the inscription 'Reader, if you seek his monument, look around you.' Herbert Mason's 1940 Blitz photograph of the cathedral surviving became an iconic image of wartime resilience.

clues.text: Rebuilt/designed after the Great Fire of London in 1666
Contains a Whispering Gallery below its dome
Sits atop Ludgate Hill in London
Wren's tomb inside reads 'Reader, if you seek his monument, look around you'
Dome inspired by Mansart's Val-de-Grace; paired Corinthian columns on facade
Woodwork by Grinling Gibbons inside
Herbert Mason's 1940 Blitz photo of it 'surviving' became iconic
Bill Viola's Martyrs and William Holman Hunt's The Light of the World housed inside
Three-shelled double dome; English Baroque style

works.name: The Monument to the Great Fire of London
works.indicator: 202-foot fluted Doric column; golden urn of fire on top; interior staircase; bombed by Martial Bourdin
works.description: Wren designed the Monument to the Great Fire of London: a 202-foot fluted Doric column with an interior staircase leading to a golden urn of fire on top. The building was later bombed by Martial Bourdin, an event described as the first international terrorist attack.

clues.text: 202-foot tall fluted Doric column
Interior staircase allowing visitors to reach the top
Topped with a golden urn of fire
Later bombed by Martial Bourdin in the first international terrorist attack

works.name: Royal Observatory, Greenwich
works.indicator: Built for John Flamsteed; Flamsteed was its first resident; marks the prime meridian
works.description: Wren designed the Royal Observatory at Greenwich to house John Flamsteed's astronomical work. Flamsteed was its first resident. Tall windows were installed in its Octagon Room. The Observatory marks the prime meridian.

clues.text: John Flamsteed was the first resident; designed to house his astronomical work
Tall windows installed in its Octagon Room
Located in Greenwich; marks the prime meridian

works.name: Other Works
works.indicator: Trinity College Library; Hampton Court Palace; Old Royal Naval College; Wren Building at William and Mary; Salisbury Cathedral repairs
works.description: Other Wren works include the library of Trinity College Cambridge; portions of Hampton Court Palace (which William and Mary tasked him to remodel to rival Versailles, a project abandoned in 1694 leaving a mix of Baroque and Tudor architecture); the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich; the Wren Building at the College of William and Mary (the oldest collegiate building in the US); and repairs to Salisbury Cathedral, where he installed hidden beams to support the tower.

clues.text: Designed the library of Trinity College Cambridge
Tasked by William and Mary to remodel Hampton Court Palace to rival Versailles; abandoned in 1694
Designed the Wren Building at William & Mary — oldest collegiate building in the US
Designed the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich
Installed hidden beams in Salisbury Cathedral to support its tower

works.name: Polymath and Other Contributions
works.indicator: Illustrated Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome; introduced the word 'neurology'; worked with Hawksmoor and Hooke
works.description: Before his architectural career, Wren created the illustrations for Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome, the book that introduced the word 'neurology.' He worked with Robert Hooke to rebuild London after the Great Fire and took on Nicholas Hawksmoor as an apprentice. He also served as MP for Weymouth, through which he controlled Portland stone quarries used for St. Paul's. John Dryden's poem Annus Mirabilis commemorates his works.

clues.text: Created illustrations for Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome, which introduced the word 'neurology'
Took on Nicholas Hawksmoor as an apprentice
Worked with Robert Hooke to rebuild London after the Great Fire
As MP for Weymouth, controlled Portland stone quarries used for St. Paul's
John Dryden's Annus Mirabilis commemorates his works
Inspired Charles Demuth's watercolor After Sir Christopher Wren
